sections.title: 1. Concept Overview
sections.body:
Facts about Carbon:
□Carbon is the 15th most abundant element in the earth's crust.
□ Earth's crust has 0.02% carbon in form of minerals like carbonates, hydrogen carbonates, coal & petroleum.
□ Carbon is present in atmosphere primarily in the form of CO₂. Its concentration is 0.03%. Also traces of CO are found.
□ The shells of aquatic animals are made from calcium carbonate.
Give a Thought:
□ Most of the things that we use daily are made up of compounds of carbon like – books, clothes, food, etc.
□ To confirm this: On burning carbon-containing compounds, a colourless & odourless gas called CO₂ is produced.
Chemical Equation:
C + O₂ CO₂
→
Confirmation Test of CO₂
(Limewater) (Calcium Carbonate)
Ca(OH)₂ = Calcium Hydroxide CaCO₃ = Calcium Carbonate
Observation:
CO₂ turns limewater turbid/milky.
Reason:
The milkiness is due to the formation of insoluble calcium carbonate (CaCO₃).
3.
Bonding in Carbon– The Covalent Bond
The bonds formed by the sharing of an electron pair between atoms are known as covalent bonds.
1. Monovalency – Hydrogen (H₂)
Hydrogen atom shares 1 electron.
H : H
•Each H-atom shares 1 electron.
•Forms a single covalent bond between two hydrogen atoms.
2. Divalency –…
**Crawled sources (internet):**
- [Carbon-and-its-Compounds class x science](https://www.slideshare.net/slideshow/carbon-and-its-compounds-class-x-science/288142605)
- [NCERT Solutions for Class 10 Science Chapter 4 Carbon and its Compounds](https://www.learncbse.in/ncert-solutions-for-class-10-science-chapter-4-carbon-and-its-compounds/)

sections.title: 2. Key Definitions
sections.body:
**Definitions extracted from crawled pages:**

- □Carbon is the 15th most abundant element in the earth's crust.
- □ Carbon is present in atmosphere primarily in the form of CO₂. Its concentration is 0.03%. Also traces of CO are found.
- □ The shells of aquatic animals are made from calcium carbonate.
- □ Most of the things that we use daily are made up of compounds of carbon like – books, clothes, food, etc.
- □ To confirm this: On burning carbon-containing compounds, a colourless & odourless gas called CO₂ is produced.
- The milkiness is due to the formation of insoluble calcium carbonate (CaCO₃).
- The bonds formed by the sharing of an electron pair between atoms are known as covalent bonds.
- C⁴⁺ is unstable as it has 6 protons in its nucleus holding only 2 electrons.

sections.title: 3. Core Concepts
sections.body:
Facts about Carbon:
□Carbon is the 15th most abundant element in the earth's crust.
□ Earth's crust has 0.02% carbon in form of minerals like carbonates, hydrogen carbonates, coal & petroleum.
□ Carbon is present in atmosphere primarily in the form of CO₂. Its concentration is 0.03%. Also traces of CO are found.
□ The shells of aquatic animals are made from calcium carbonate.
Give a Thought:
□ Most of the things that we use daily are made up of compounds of carbon like – books, clothes, food, etc.
□ To confirm this: On burning carbon-containing compounds, a colourless & odourless gas called CO₂ is produced.
Chemical Equation:
C + O₂ CO₂
→
Confirmation Test of CO₂
(Limewater) (Calcium Carbonate)
Ca(OH)₂ = Calcium Hydroxide CaCO₃ = Calcium Carbonate
Observation:
CO₂ turns limewater turbid/milky.
Reason:
The milkiness is due to the formation of insoluble calcium carbonate (CaCO₃).
3.
Bonding in Carbon– The Covalent Bond
The bonds formed by the sharing of an electron pair between atoms are known as covalent bonds.
1. Monovalency – Hydrogen (H₂)
Hydrogen atom shares 1 electron.
H : H
•Each H-atom shares 1 electron.
•Forms a single covalent bond between two hydrogen atoms.
2. Divalency – Oxygen (O₂)
Oxygen atom shares 2 electrons.
O :: O
•Each O-atom shares 2 electrons.
•Forms a double covalent bond between two oxygen atoms.
3. Trivalency – Nitrogen (N₂)
Nitrogen atom shares 3 electrons.
N N
≡
Each N-atom shares 3 electrons.
Forms a triple covalent bond between two nitrogen atoms.
4.
Valency Example ElectronsShared Bond Type
Monovalency Hydrogen (H₂) 1 Single Covalent Bond
Divalency Oxygen (O₂) 2 Double Covalent Bond
Trivalency Nitrogen (N₂) 3 Triple Covalent Bond
Summary Table
Case of Carbon
•Carbon has 4 electrons in its outermost shell.
Carbon cannot gain 4 electrons
•Due to the small size of the carbon atom, 6 protons of carbon cannot hold 10 electrons.
•Repulsion between electrons increases.
Carbon cannot lose 4 electrons
•It would require a large amount of energy to…

sections.title: 4. Formulas / Theorems
sections.body:
**Formulas / relations found:**

- Ca(OH)₂ = Calcium Hydroxide CaCO₃ = Calcium Carbonate
- In CH3Cl : C = 6, H = 1 and Cl = 17 And their electronic configuration is C – 2,4, H – 1 and Cl – 2, 8, 7
- For butanol, n = 4. So, formula is
- (d) Propene, CH3CH=CH2 (ii) and propyne, CH3— C = CH (iii) both have double and triple bonds, respectively, hence are unsaturated
- (b) Electronic configuration of carbon (C) = 2, 4 when it forms four covalent bonds by sharing its four valence electrons wi
- Atomic number of carbon = 6
- E.g. CH2 = CH2 + H2 + (Nickel catalyst) → CH3– CH3

sections.title: 5. Solved Examples
sections.body:
**Example snippets:**

- Valency Example ElectronsShared Bond Type
- For example, acidic K2Cr2O7 is an oxidising agent, that converts (oxidises) ethanol into ethanoic acid.
- What is a homologous series ? Explain with an example.
- (i) All members of a homologous series can be represented by the same general formula. For example, the general formula of the homologous series of alkanes is CnH2n+2, in which ‘n’ denotes number of carbon and hydrogen atoms in one molecule of alkane.
- For example, general formula of the homologous series of alkanes is CnH2n+2, in which ‘n’ denotes number of carbon atoms in one molecule of alkane. Following are the first five members of the homologous series of alkanes (general formula CnH2n+2).
- What is a homologous series? Explain with an example.

sections.title: 8. Practice Questions
sections.body:
**Practice-style lines from sources:**

- Is the covalent bond a weaker bond?
- Why Carbon ShowsCatenation to the Maximum Extent?
- What would be the electron dot structure of carbon dioxide which has the formula CO2 ?
- How many structural isomers can you draw for pentane ?
- What are the two properties of carbon which lead to the huge number of carbon compounds we see around us ?
- What will be the formula and electron dot structure of cyclopentane ?
- How would you name the following compounds ?
- Why is the conversion of ethanol to ethanoic acid an oxidation reaction ?
- A mixture of oxygen and ethyne is burnt for welding. Can you tell why a mixture of ethyne and air is not used ?
- How would you distinguish experimentally between an alcohol and a carboxylic acid ?

sections.title: 9. Real World Applications
sections.body:
**Applications mentioned:**

- □ Most of the things that we use daily are made up of compounds of carbon like – books, clothes, food, etc.
- What is hydrogenation ? What is its industrial application ?
- Application : The process of hydrogenation has an important industrial application. It is used to prepare vegetable ghee (or vanaspati ghee) from vegetable oils.
- What is hydrogenation? What is its industrial application?
